title: Pleuritic chest pain in a dialysis patient who missed his last two sessions

chart.cc: Sharp chest pain and feeling unwell
chart.oneLiner: 58M with ESRD on hemodialysis (recently non-adherent), type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and prior CABG presents with 3 days of sharp, positional, pleuritic central chest pain that is worse lying flat and better leaning forward, after missing his last two dialysis sessions.

opening: I've had this sharp pain in the middle of my chest for a few days. It's worse when I lie down, and it eases up if I sit forward. I haven't been able to get to my dialysis the last couple of times.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 58-year-old man, looks tired and a little puffy, sitting leaning forward on the stretcher. Cooperative but worried because the pain reminds him of his heart bypass years ago.

HPI:
- Sharp, central/retrosternal chest pain for 3 days.
- POSITIONAL: worse lying supine, better sitting up and leaning forward. PLEURITIC: worse with deep breath and with coughing.
- Sometimes radiates to the left trapezius ridge / top of the left shoulder (only if asked where it travels).
- NOT the same as his old angina before bypass: that was a dull pressure with exertion that went away with rest; THIS is sharp, constant, and unrelated to walking (only if asked to compare).
- Missed his last TWO dialysis sessions (about a week without dialysis) — he was traveling for a family funeral and could not get a chair (reveal only if asked about dialysis schedule/adherence or recent changes).
- Mild shortness of breath and his legs/face feel more swollen than usual; has been more fatigued and a bit nauseated (only if asked about associated symptoms).
- Had a head-cold a couple of weeks ago that resolved (only if asked about recent illness).
- NO tearing/ripping pain to the back, NO syncope, NO unilateral leg swelling or calf pain, NO recent long travel by car/plane beyond the bus to the funeral, NO hemoptysis.

OLDCARTS: onset 3 days ago; central chest; constant with pleuritic/positional exacerbation; sharp; worse supine and with inspiration, better leaning forward; severity 6/10; associated with mild dyspnea, increased edema, nausea, fatigue.

PMH: End-stage renal disease on hemodialysis (Mon/Wed/Fri, via a left-arm AV fistula) for ~4 years, secondary to diabetic nephropathy. Type 2 diabetes (~15 years). Hypertension. Coronary artery disease s/p CABG 6 years ago. Hyperlipidemia. Anemia of CKD. NO known autoimmune disease.
MEDICATIONS (polypharmacy — reveal the list if asked what he takes): aspirin 81 mg daily; metoprolol; lisinopril; atorvastatin; insulin glargine + lispro; sevelamer (phosphate binder); calcitriol; erythropoietin injections at dialysis; recently started naproxen (an over-the-counter NSAID) for knee pain about 10 days ago. He sometimes skips his evening meds when he travels. NO warfarin or DOAC.
ALLERGIES: none. SOCIAL: former smoker (quit 8 years ago, ~30 pack-years), rare alcohol, retired bus mechanic, lives with his wife. FAMILY: father had an MI in his 60s. ROS: as above; mild dyspnea and increased edema; no fever chills beyond feeling warm; no urinary output to speak of (anuric on dialysis).

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY the system/maneuver requested; give normals when asked):
- General: tired-appearing, mildly volume-overloaded, more comfortable leaning forward.
- Vitals: BP 158/92, HR 104, RR 20, 37.7°C, SpO2 96% RA.
- Cardiac: a three-component PERICARDIAL FRICTION RUB is audible at the left lower sternal border, best with the patient leaning forward in end-expiration; normal S1/S2, no new murmur; PMI not displaced.
- Neck/JVP: JVP mildly elevated (~9 cm); check for pulsus paradoxus — only a borderline/normal pulsus (<10 mmHg) is appreciated, no clear tamponade physiology.
- Lungs: a few bibasilar crackles; otherwise clear; no consolidation.
- Chest wall: NO reproducible tenderness over the sternum or costochondral joints; healed midline sternotomy scar.
- Access: left-forearm AV fistula with a palpable thrill and audible bruit (patent).
- Extremities: mild-to-moderate bilateral pitting pedal edema (chronic, slightly worse); no unilateral swelling, no calf tenderness. Abdomen: soft, nontender.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis.
- Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the missed dialysis sessions, the recent naproxen, the trapezius radiation, or the comparison to his old angina unless asked the relevant question (dialysis schedule/adherence, medications, where the pain travels, or how it compares to prior chest pain).
- The friction rub is only revealed if the student auscultates the heart (ideally with the patient leaning forward).
- Reveal the elevated JVP / pulsus check only if the student examines the neck / checks for pulsus paradoxus.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.CBC: WBC 11.2 (mildly elevated), Hgb 9.4 (chronic anemia of CKD, at his baseline), Plt 244.
labs.BMP: Na 138, K 6.2 (HIGH), Cl 100, HCO3 16 (LOW), BUN 92, Cr 7.8 (up from his dialysis baseline of ~6.0), glucose 184. Calculated anion gap = Na − (Cl + HCO3) = 138 − (100 + 16) = 22 (ELEVATED, high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis from missed dialysis / uremia).
labs.Troponin: High-sensitivity troponin mildly elevated and STABLE on serial draws (0.09 then 0.10 ng/mL) without a rising/falling pattern — consistent with myopericardial involvement (myopericarditis) and chronic elevation from CKD, NOT a dynamic ACS curve.
labs.Inflammatory: ESR and CRP elevated. Phosphate high at 6.8, corrected calcium 8.1 (low-normal) — consistent with missed dialysis.
labs.echo: Small-to-moderate circumferential pericardial effusion WITHOUT echocardiographic tamponade (no RV diastolic collapse, IVC not plethoric, respiratory mitral inflow variation <25%); normal-to-mildly reduced LV function with old inferior wall-motion abnormality from prior CABG; no new regional wall-motion abnormality to suggest acute coronary occlusion.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential diagnosis (note your leading diagnosis). Then name three distinct etiologies/mechanisms of acute pericarditis that you would specifically consider in THIS patient.
steps.rubric: Acute pericarditis / myopericarditis (leading, accepts pericarditis; 7). Acute coronary syndrome / NSTEMI — must-not-miss given prior CABG, diabetes, mild troponin rise, and an EKG that can mimic STEMI (CANT-MISS, 5). Pulmonary embolism — must-not-miss given pleuritic pain and immobility (CANT-MISS, 3). Aortic dissection (CANT-MISS, 1). Pneumonia/pleuritis (core, 1). Names pericarditis with supporting features — positional pleuritic pain, friction rub, missed dialysis/uremia (core, 3). Names THREE distinct etiologies/mechanisms relevant here — e.g. UREMIC (missed dialysis), viral/idiopathic (recent URI), post-cardiac-injury/Dressler after prior CABG, autoimmune, NSAID-related is NOT an etiology (core, 5). PENALTY: anchors on simple viral pericarditis and ignores the uremic/ACS picture (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Acute pericarditis with myocardial involvement (myopericarditis). Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Acute coronary syndrome / NSTEMI (he has prior CABG, diabetes, and a mild troponin rise, and the EKG can mimic STEMI); Pulmonary embolism; Aortic dissection. Also reasonable: pneumonia/pleuritis. Three etiologies/mechanisms to consider specifically in this man: (1) UREMIC pericarditis from missed dialysis / accumulated uremic toxins; (2) viral or idiopathic pericarditis following his recent upper-respiratory illness; (3) post-cardiac-injury syndrome (Dressler-type) given his prior CABG, or an autoimmune/connective-tissue process.

coreActions.item: Names pericarditis with supporting features — positional pleuritic pain, friction rub, missed dialysis/uremia
Names three distinct etiologies/mechanisms — uremic (missed dialysis), viral/idiopathic, post-cardiac-injury (Dressler) / autoimmune

penalties.item: Anchors on simple viral pericarditis and ignores the uremic/ACS picture

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: EKG immediately (CANT-MISS, 4). BMP / electrolytes — he missed dialysis, look for hyperkalemia and acidosis (CANT-MISS, 3). SERIAL troponins to distinguish myopericarditis/chronic CKD elevation from a dynamic ACS curve (core, 2). Echocardiogram to assess for effusion/tamponade (core, 2). Inflammatory markers ESR/CRP (core, 1). CBC (core, 1). CXR (core, 1). PENALTY: no EKG (-2); reflexively giving an NSAID before reviewing renal function/potassium (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG immediately; BMP/electrolytes (hyperkalemia and metabolic acidosis are expected after two missed dialysis sessions); SERIAL high-sensitivity troponins (a flat, non-dynamic curve favors myopericarditis/chronic CKD elevation over ACS); CBC; ESR/CRP; phosphate/calcium; CXR; transthoracic echocardiogram to look for an effusion and tamponade physiology. Notify the renal/dialysis team early.

criticalActions.item: BMP / electrolytes (hyperkalemia and acidosis after missed dialysis)

coreActions.item: Serial troponins to distinguish myopericarditis/CKD elevation from ACS

penalties.item: Gives an NSAID before checking renal function/potassium

requiredTests: BMP / electrolytes (hyperkalemia and acidosis after missed dialysis)
optionalTests: Serial troponins to distinguish myopericarditis/CKD elevation from ACS
contraindicatedTests: Gives an NSAID before checking renal function/potassium

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = diffuse concave-upward ST elevation with PR-segment depression and PR elevation/reciprocal change in aVR (Spodick's down-sloping TP segment may be seen); rate ~104, sinus. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (3); identifies WIDESPREAD (non-territorial) ST elevation with PR depression (6); distinguishes from STEMI - diffuse rather than a single coronary territory, no reciprocal ST depression except aVR/V1 (4); systematic read, comments on T waves given the hyperkalemia (2). PENALTY: misreading as an acute STEMI and activating the cath lab (-3).
keyFindings: distinguishes from STEMI - diffuse rather than a single coronary territory
criticalFindings: Diffuse concave-upward ST-segment elevation across multiple leads with PR-segment depression; PR elevation / reciprocal change in aVR; sinus tachycardia.
commonMistakes: misreading as an acute STEMI and activating the cath lab
missing peaked T waves of hyperkalemia in a patient who skipped dialysis
steps.idealAnswer: Diffuse concave-upward ST-segment elevation across multiple leads with PR-segment depression; PR elevation with reciprocal change in aVR; Spodick's sign (down-sloping TP segment) may be present. Sinus tachycardia at ~104. In a patient who missed dialysis, also scrutinize the T waves for peaking from hyperkalemia.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Diffuse concave-upward ST-segment elevation across multiple leads with PR-segment depression; reciprocal change in aVR.

coreActions.item: distinguishes from STEMI - diffuse rather than a single coronary territory

penalties.item: misreading as an acute STEMI and activating the cath lab

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = enlarged, globular ('water-bottle') cardiac silhouette consistent with a pericardial effusion, with mild pulmonary vascular congestion/small effusions from volume overload after missed dialysis; clear of focal consolidation. CREDIT: identifies the enlarged globular cardiac silhouette suggesting a pericardial effusion (6); recognizes signs of volume overload / vascular congestion in a missed-dialysis patient (4). PENALTY: calling the study normal / missing the abnormality (-3).
criticalFindings: Enlarged globular ('water-bottle') cardiac silhouette consistent with a pericardial effusion, with mild congestion from volume overload.
commonMistakes: mistaking the effusion silhouette for simple cardiomegaly
calling the film normal in a fluid-overloaded dialysis patient
steps.idealAnswer: Enlarged, globular 'water-bottle' cardiac silhouette consistent with a pericardial effusion, with mild pulmonary vascular congestion and small pleural effusions reflecting volume overload after missed dialysis; no focal consolidation.

criticalActions: Enlarged globular ('water-bottle') cardiac silhouette consistent with a pericardial effusion, with mild congestion from volume overload.

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given the diffuse ST elevation with PR depression on the pinned EKG, the stable mild troponin, the hyperkalemia and anion-gap acidosis, and the small effusion on echo, what is your single most likely diagnosis? Name the test that best discriminates it from your top competitor, and state any residual uncertainty.
steps.rubric: Commits to acute pericarditis with myocardial involvement (myopericarditis), MOST LIKELY UREMIC given two missed dialysis sessions with hyperkalemia and high-anion-gap acidosis (8). Names the discriminator from ACS — diffuse (non-territorial) ST elevation with PR depression on the EKG plus a FLAT serial troponin (no rise/fall) and no new regional wall-motion abnormality on echo, rather than a dynamic ACS curve (5). Notes the small effusion without tamponade, and acknowledges residual uncertainty — overlapping viral/idiopathic or post-CABG (Dressler) etiology, and that a clearly dynamic troponin would still mandate ACS workup (3).
steps.idealAnswer: Acute pericarditis with myocardial involvement (myopericarditis), most likely UREMIC given two missed hemodialysis sessions with hyperkalemia (K 6.2) and a high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis (AG 22). The diffuse concave ST elevation with PR depression on the pinned pericarditis EKG, together with a FLAT (non-dynamic) serial troponin and no new regional wall-motion abnormality on echo, discriminate it from ACS/NSTEMI; the absence of RV diastolic collapse and IVC plethora confirms a small effusion WITHOUT tamponade. Residual uncertainty: a viral/idiopathic contribution from his recent URI or a post-cardiac-injury (Dressler) component from prior CABG cannot be fully excluded, and a clearly rising/falling troponin would still mandate an ischemic workup.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan? Address how the uremic etiology and his renal failure change first-line therapy, and state your disposition — specifically whether this patient is safe for discharge.
steps.rubric: Per MGH pericardial / dialysis pericarditis: URGENT/INTENSIFIED hemodialysis as the definitive treatment of uremic pericarditis — arrange dialysis now (CANT-MISS, 7); treat the hyperkalemia (calcium gluconate to stabilize the membrane, insulin/dextrose, and dialysis) (CANT-MISS, 5); recognize that high-dose NSAIDs are RELATIVELY CONTRAINDICATED in ESRD/uremic pericarditis and that colchicine must be dose-reduced/often avoided in renal failure (core, 4); echocardiography and serial exams to watch for tamponade (core, 3); reconcile medications — STOP the recently started naproxen (core, 2); ADMIT — this patient is NOT safe for discharge: pericarditis with myocardial involvement + an effusion + uremia + hyperkalemia are high-risk features requiring inpatient dialysis and monitoring; safe-discharge planning only after dialysis, with nephrology follow-up and dialysis-adherence counseling (core, 3). PENALTY: discharging this high-risk patient (-4); high-dose NSAID/colchicine as if normal renal function (-3); corticosteroids first-line (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: The definitive treatment of UREMIC pericarditis is URGENT, INTENSIFIED hemodialysis — arrange dialysis now (he has missed two sessions). Treat the hyperkalemia in parallel: IV calcium gluconate to stabilize the myocardium, insulin with dextrose (and a beta-agonist) to shift potassium, and dialysis for definitive removal; recheck the potassium. Do NOT reach for high-dose NSAIDs — they are relatively contraindicated in ESRD/uremic pericarditis — and use colchicine cautiously or not at all because it requires dose reduction and accumulates in renal failure; reserve corticosteroids for refractory/specific etiologies. Reconcile his medications and STOP the recently started naproxen. Echocardiography and serial exams to monitor for tamponade. DISPOSITION: ADMIT — this patient is NOT safe for discharge. Pericarditis with myocardial involvement, a pericardial effusion, hyperkalemia, and uremia from missed dialysis are high-risk features that mandate inpatient dialysis and cardiac monitoring. Plan safe discharge only after dialysis and clinical improvement, with close nephrology/cardiology follow-up and counseling on dialysis adherence.

criticalActions.item: Urgent/intensified hemodialysis as definitive treatment of uremic pericarditis

coreActions.item: Recognizes NSAIDs relatively contraindicated and colchicine must be dose-reduced/avoided in renal failure
Echocardiography and serial exams to monitor for tamponade
Medication reconciliation — stop the recently started naproxen
Admit — NOT safe for discharge; high-risk features; safe-discharge planning only after dialysis with nephrology follow-up and adherence counseling

bonusActions.item: Treat underlying cause / address dialysis-adherence barriers

penalties.item: High-dose NSAID/colchicine as if normal renal function

requiredActions: Urgent/intensified hemodialysis as definitive treatment of uremic pericarditis
optionalActions: Recognizes NSAIDs relatively contraindicated and colchicine must be dose-reduced/avoided in renal failure
Echocardiography and serial exams to monitor for tamponade
Medication reconciliation — stop the recently started naproxen
Admit — NOT safe for discharge; high-risk features; safe-discharge planning only after dialysis with nephrology follow-up and adherence counseling
Treat underlying cause / address dialysis-adherence barriers
unsafeActions: High-dose NSAID/colchicine as if normal renal function
steps.disposition: Admit for inpatient dialysis and cardiac monitoring; not safe for discharge

physicalExamMappings.finding: Cardiac: a three-component PERICARDIAL FRICTION RUB is audible at the left lower sternal border, best with the patient leaning forward in end-expiration; normal S1/S2, no new murmur; PMI not displaced.
Neck/JVP: JVP mildly elevated (~9 cm); pulsus paradoxus borderline/normal (<10 mmHg), no clear tamponade physiology.
Lungs: a few bibasilar crackles consistent with mild volume overload; otherwise clear, no consolidation.
Extremities: mild-to-moderate bilateral pitting pedal edema (chronic, slightly worse); no unilateral swelling, no calf tenderness.
Chest wall: NO reproducible tenderness over the sternum or costochondral joints; healed midline sternotomy scar.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 58M with ESRD on hemodialysis (recently non-adherent — missed his last two sessions), type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and prior CABG presents with 3 days of sharp, positional, pleuritic chest pain better leaning forward, with a friction rub. The diagnosis is Acute pericarditis with myocardial involvement (myopericarditis), most likely UREMIC, on a background of hyperkalemia and a high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis from missed dialysis. It is reached by eliciting the focused history (including dialysis adherence and the recently started naproxen), performing the targeted exam, ordering electrolytes and serial troponins, interpreting a pericarditis EKG and a 'water-bottle' CXR, and excluding the must-not-miss alternatives (ACS/NSTEMI, PE, dissection) — then treating with urgent dialysis rather than the usual NSAID/colchicine because of his renal failure.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Cardiac: a three-component PERICARDIAL FRICTION RUB is audible at the left lower sternal border, best with the patient leaning forward in end-expiration; normal S1/S2, no new murmur; PMI not displaced.
Neck/JVP: JVP mildly elevated (~9 cm); pulsus paradoxus borderline/normal (<10 mmHg), no clear tamponade physiology.
Lungs: a few bibasilar crackles consistent with mild volume overload; otherwise clear, no consolidation.
Extremities: mild-to-moderate bilateral pitting pedal edema (chronic, slightly worse); no unilateral swelling, no calf tenderness.
Chest wall: NO reproducible tenderness over the sternum or costochondral joints; healed midline sternotomy scar.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including dialysis adherence, recent NSAID use, and the comparison to prior angina, only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal the friction rub, JVP, and volume status.
Build a differential led by acute pericarditis (myopericarditis) while explicitly excluding ACS/NSTEMI, PE, and dissection, and name uremic vs viral/idiopathic vs post-injury etiologies.
Order electrolytes and serial troponins, choose echocardiography, and interpret the pericarditis EKG and the 'water-bottle' CXR.
Commit to uremic pericarditis and manage with urgent dialysis and hyperkalemia treatment — NOT high-dose NSAIDs/colchicine — and admit (not safe for discharge).
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on simple viral pericarditis and missing the uremic/ACS picture
Reflexively giving high-dose NSAIDs or full-dose colchicine in renal failure
Discharging a high-risk dialysis patient with an effusion and hyperkalemia
Misreading the diffuse ST elevation as a STEMI and activating the cath lab
managementPearls: Urgent/intensified hemodialysis as definitive treatment of uremic pericarditis
criticalWorkupItems: BMP / electrolytes (hyperkalemia and acidosis after missed dialysis)
criticalInterpretationItems: Diffuse concave-upward ST-segment elevation across multiple leads with PR-segment depression; reciprocal change in aVR.
Enlarged globular ('water-bottle') cardiac silhouette consistent with a pericardial effusion, with mild congestion from volume overload.
criticalManagementItems: Urgent/intensified hemodialysis as definitive treatment of uremic pericarditis
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Diffuse concave-upward ST-segment elevation across multiple leads with PR-segment depression; reciprocal change in aVR.
Enlarged globular ('water-bottle') cardiac silhouette consistent with a pericardial effusion, with mild congestion from volume overload.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Diffuse concave-upward ST-segment elevation across multiple leads with PR-segment depression; reciprocal change in aVR.
Enlarged globular ('water-bottle') cardiac silhouette consistent with a pericardial effusion, with mild congestion from volume overload.
imageInterpretationPearls: Diffuse (non-territorial) ST elevation with PR depression is pericarditis, not a STEMI — confirm with the clinical picture before activating the cath lab.
In a missed-dialysis patient, scrutinize the T waves for hyperkalemic peaking even when the dominant pattern is pericarditic.
An enlarged globular 'water-bottle' heart on CXR suggests a pericardial effusion; read every study systematically and state pertinent negatives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern that this is his heart again
Asks non-judgmentally about dialysis adherence and missed sessions
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
